questions.question: Why do training and inference need different optimizations?
options: They run on different hardware vendors

Training is parallel and FLOP-bound; inference is serial and MEMORY-bound — a naive decoder does O(N²) work recomputing attention over the full prefix at every step, and attention's HBM traffic underuses GPUs 4-10x
Inference uses a different loss
Training never touches attention
questions.explanation: Different bottleneck, different tricks: the KV cache attacks redundant computation; Flash Attention attacks redundant memory movement.

questions.question: What does the KV cache exploit?
options: Tokens repeating in the vocabulary
Every prefix token's K and V vectors are deterministic once computed — store them, and each new token needs only ONE query against the cached keys, dropping per-step cost from O(N²) to O(N)
Sparsity in the attention matrix
Quantization of model weights
questions.explanation: For a 4K-token response, naive recomputation is 16M attention operations, almost all redundant — the cache is the single most important inference optimization.

questions.question: How does Flash Attention achieve its speedup?
options: By skipping the softmax entirely
Tiling: the full N×N score matrix NEVER hits HBM — softmax and matmuls happen block-by-block in on-chip SRAM, giving 2-4x wall-clock on A100 and 5-10x on H100 with FP8
By pruning low attention scores
By caching attention outputs to disk
questions.explanation: Dao et al. It computes the same exact attention — no approximation — just with a memory-hierarchy-aware schedule. Universal in every 2026 inference stack.

questions.question: How big does the KV cache get, and how does GQA help?
options: A few megabytes regardless of model

Llama 3 70B (80 layers, 8 KV heads via GQA) needs ~10.4 GB for a 32K context at batch 1 — with full 64-head MHA it would be 32 GB; GQA's shared K/V heads cut the cache by the head-grouping factor, and MLA compresses further
It's constant per token count
GQA increases cache size for quality
questions.explanation: The arithmetic (2 × heads × d_head × dtype bytes per token per layer) is why attention-variant choice is really a serving-economics decision.

questions.question: Why does the production stack assume both optimizations?
options: Marketing requirements
vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp all build on KV caching and Flash Attention as the baseline — without them, long-context serving is memory-starved and latency-bound beyond usability
They are required by the model license
Only one of them is ever needed
questions.explanation: These two Dao-era optimizations moved frontier inference from "slow" to "fast" and became the floor every further optimization (paged attention, prefix caching) stands on.
